text: The surgical field shows no visible signs of liver metastasis or peritoneal disease.
The DaVinci XI robotic system is docked between the patient's legs, positioning the robotic arms for the colorectal procedure.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum medial to the right iliac vessels, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon mobilizes the mesentery from the retroperitoneum using a medial approach, with robotic assistance visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter, visually distinguishing it from surrounding tissues during the colorectal procedure.
The surgeon continues medial dissection behind the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and initiates lymphadenectomy at the IMA origin, using precise techniques to separate lymphatic tissue.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, aligning it with the previously completed medial dissection, using robotic assistance from a docked position between the legs.
The surgeon completes the lymphadenectomy and identifies the superior rectal artery and left colic artery, with these structures clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler with a vascular load to transect the superior rectal artery, completing the vascular division.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to divide the colonic mesentery, followed by transection of the sigmoid colon with a robotic stapler.
The surgeon completes the dissection, freeing the upper specimen from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs posterior total mesorectal excision (TME), carefully dissecting within the mesorectum envelope down to the pelvic floor, ensuring precise separation of tissue layers.
As the dissection progresses deeper into the pelvis, the rectum shifts to a more anterior position, revealing its orientation relative to surrounding structures.
The video shows fibrotic tissues resulting from previous radiation therapy, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon mobilizes the rectum circumferentially along the right and left rectal walls, ensuring complete separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues anterior dissection to remove the uterus and vagina en bloc with the rectum, with visible assistance from the gynecology oncology team.
The surgeon divides the round ligaments bilaterally, using surgical instruments to cut through these structures on both sides.
The surgeon opens and develops the retroperitoneal space, revealing the anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon isolates, coagulates, and divides the infundibulopelvic ligaments bilaterally, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects the bladder down anteriorly by approximately four to five centimeters, creating a clear separation from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine vessels, followed by coagulation and division of these vessels, as part of the ongoing dissection process.
The surgeon inserts a uterine manipulator into the endometrial cavity prior to making an incision.
The surgeon takes additional sutures through the cardinal ligaments towards the uterine manipulator, securing the ligaments in place.
The surgeon carefully separates the vagina from the bladder distally, ensuring that both ureters remain intact and undisturbed during the dissection.
The surgeon transects the levator muscles across the right, left, and posterior planes, completing the separation of these structures.
The surgeon dissects the anterior plane between the bladder and the vagina, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon removes the uterine manipulator from the endometrial cavity, followed by the undocking of the robotic surgical system.
The surgical team focuses on completing the perineal dissection, carefully maneuvering to ensure precise separation of the tissue layers in the perineal region.
The surgeon makes an elliptical incision encompassing the perianal skin and the posterior right and left walls of the vagina, carefully cutting through these areas.
The surgeon continues the dissection into the ischioanal and ischiorectal space, further separating the tissue planes in these regions.
The surgeon begins reconstructing the vaginal and perineal wounds, focusing on repairing and closing the incised areas.
A longitudinal incision is made through the left medial posterior thigh, exposing the gracilis muscle.
The surgeon divides the distal portion of the gracilis muscle and elevates it from its bed, moving in a distal to proximal direction.
The surgeon creates a counterincision and transposes the immobilized muscle flap into the perineal defect, completing the reconstruction process.
The surgeon sutures the distal ends of two muscles together, completing the connection between them within the surgical field.
The surgeon inverts the muscle flaps into the perineal wound and secures them to the surrounding tissue, completing the reconstruction of the area.
The video shows direct invasion of the colorectal disease into the vaginal tissue, highlighting the affected area.
